pattern: Stripping the zeros out of a number's digits always yields a strictly shorter number that is no larger than the original, and any zero-free number at most n can always be produced by stripping itself -- so the answer is simply how many integers up to n contain no zero digit at all.
hints: Whenever a number has at least one zero digit, deleting those zeros shortens its decimal representation, and a number with fewer digits is always smaller than one with more (assuming no leading zero) -- so the stripped result never exceeds n.

Conversely, any zero-free number y that is at most n is trivially produced by taking x = y itself, since stripping zeros from a number with none does nothing -- so every zero-free value up to n is achievable, and nothing else is.

The problem therefore reduces to counting how many integers from 1 to n contain no zero digit, which you can compute digit by digit: count all shorter zero-free lengths outright, then walk n's own digits left to right allowing a strictly smaller nonzero digit at the first point of difference.

edge_inputs.description: n itself contains a zero digit
n itself has no zero digit
n is an exact power of ten
n is one less than a power of ten, all digits nines
n has a zero in the middle, not just trailing
n has zeros in almost every position
a two-digit n just above a round number

stress.description: random n across the full magnitude range, including near powers of ten
